We usually use pytest's `monkeypatch` fixture instead of `patch` in the test suite (although not always...).
The advantage is that the monkeypatch fixture is integrated with pytest and also defaults to raising an exception if the patch target doesn't exist (e.g. after a refactoring) which is not the case with `patch`.

I think it would be nicer to put this into a fixture and / or test helper (e.g. `disable_encrypted_secret`) so as not having to repeat this patch boilerplate on many tests (which would need to be changed if ever the `decrypt_secret` function was moved or otherwise refactored).

Also this only tests the branch where the included secret is invalid, not the one where none is provided in the first place.

Hi @lloeki, I was thinking about the same issues, but got different conclusions. @BaptisteFoy @arbll - maybe you can help us figure this out.

Lets start with how RC backend handle state. the backend is stateless and respects the state given by the client. compare the given state to the desired state and send back updated configs version if needed. So starting a new client-id with "non-empty" state is ok, as that is how the backend handle any requests.

For DI for example, keeping the state this "right" thing to do as all loaded probes (configurations) are already applied on the the forked process code (wrapped functions). Starting an "empty" state client would mean to unwrap all those functions and then reapply them when the backend send the same configurations. 

Maybe on ASM this is different, and we always need to start with an empty state? for example, IP blocking roles - I think it better to reuse already loaded roles on the forked process, no?

For me, it all about whether the forked process is the "same" services as its parent. if it is, then keep the state the client reduces load on both the backend and forked process as many of those configurations would be needed to apply. If the forked process is a different services than those configurations might no longer be relevant and clearing the state would be the right thing to do. And I'm not familiar enough in Python or Ruby to know which option is more common.

> AFAICS there are two sets of files where straightforward clash detection won't work: a) AlternativeImages (which might be many with the same pageId, mimetype and filegrp) and b) document-wide documents like TEI or PDF that do not have a pageId.
> 
> To accomodate a), I've added a new kwarg `is_alternative_image` that, if set, will ignore the "grp/mimetype/pageId" check outright. Unless the IDs are clashing, it's perfectly valid to have many files with the same mimetype in the same fileGrp for the same pageId.

I see two problems with that:
1. you force the caller to decide in advance what the file is for. Hard to decide if you are in a shell script. (Basically, this extra arg has to be dragged through everywhere, with lots of places to adapt.)
2. there may be other multi-file-per-page cases besides derived images. Take custom formats like JSON or txt per region/line/word/glyph for example. We already have these cases in existing processors (e.g. ocrd-segment-extract-lines, ocrd-segment-replace-text).

Like I said, in all cases except PAGE-XML we simply do not know in advance whether multi-file semantics is ok or not. My formulation was more general and should not need that distinction. However, it hinges on the assumption that the malign case (ID clash from another, unrelated process) does not coincide with a grp/mime/page clash. (Which in my opinion is safe to make.)

> The files in b) have in common that they lack a pageId. So, in case there is a "grp/mimetype" clash without `pageId` being set, nothing is removed. This does prevent e.g. having two vartiants of TEI of the whole document in the same fileGrp. So if that is a realistic prospect, it needs another round of adaptions.

Yes, I agree we can assume that `pageId=None` is just as unique as any single pageId. But then you don't need the extra check. Notice this is already covered by my pseudocode (which makes for fewer conditionals).

Looks like it's not issue of `await`; it's more like unnoticed bug being revealed. What `aiohttp` complaing about is that request body is not a json. Current implementation makes `decrypt_payload()` middleware to do its job on all the requests coming into webserver. And since your middleware is trying to treat all request bodies (which aren't empty) as json, when a request which isn't json (maybe graphql requests?), boom. It'll fail like this.    
IMO there is no reason to try to decode & re-encode request body as json. Let's just keep it as `str`.     
Also, dict key which holds decrypted payload should be some other self-explanatory name, not `creds`.

   """
   Returns a jwt_decode_handler that will only validate asymmetrically signed JWTs.

       jwt_decode_handler from this library. This can be used to decode an
       already decoded JWT, to ensure it is asymmetrically signed. This check
       can go away once the DEPR for symmetrically signed JWTs is complete:

   """

Comments:
1. There's no need to provide numerical values on the order of 1e-12, since we only run tests to 2 or 6 decimal places. So the small values here should be explicitly zeroed out.
2. If you're testing nontrivial functionality then you should explain how you got the answer ``expect_T``. Only a small portion of our tests actually do this, but I think it's important.

Other points
1. Your constraints on ``a_diag`` and ``b_diag`` should make sure they don't all take the same values at optimality. Right now that's happening, and it's an uninteresting test since if a symmetric matrix only has one distinct eigenvalue then that matrix must be a multiple of the identity.
2. You can try computing compute the reference value ``T_expect`` by having ``T_expect = U @ diag(t_expect) @ U.T``, and then using scalar relative entropy (exact, not quad approx) to compute the optimal value of ``t_expect``.

Maybe we should move these requirements into the `setup.py` `setup()`'s `extras_require` kwarg. They seem like tack-on extra functionality / features so they meet the use case of that kwarg.

It could be implemented something like this:
```python
setup(
    # other code....
    extras_require={
        "patch_filtering": [
            "whatthepatch",
        ],
        "coverage_filtering": [
            "coverage",
        ],
    },
)
```
That way if someone wants the enable the coverage filtering they could install it via pip with:

```shell
pip install mutmut["coverage_filtering"]
```

And if they wanted to enable patch filtering they could install via pip with:
```shell
pip install mutmut["patch_filtering"]
```

This should help limit core dependencies and lower the chance of dependency conflict with other peoples projects.

The point is perhaps moot because `get_token` is usually called by an auth policy which caches access tokens itself. There will typically be a period of minutes--during which authorization will succeed--between a user signing out and the next `get_token` call.

Still, you ask a good question. Here's a couple things to think about in answering it:
1. Access tokens aren't connected to refresh tokens. They're valid for their (short) lifetimes even after the refresh token used to acquire them expires or is revoked.
1. In this scenario the user has signed out but left a program running. The program has tried to send a service request. Which is more surprising to the user: the request succeeding, or failing?

There's another interesting case here. The user can change the signed in identity between two calls to `get_token`. That suggests an argument for having `get_token` always redeem the refresh token, if any, for a new access token, although I don't think users should expect changing identities midstream to pass without error. Redeeming the refresh token every time also raises another issue: when we redeem a refresh token, AAD may return us a new one and invalidate the one we sent. We don't contribute this new refresh token back to VS Code. That means our credential can invalidate the Azure Account extension's refresh token but itself continue to succeed and I guess we then recurse on "what happens if the user signs out of the extension?" 😸 

I'm trying to get all the food for thought here down on this page, sorry for all the words. Personally I now lean toward:
1. forget caching in this credential; leave it up to the auth policy
1. if the user signs out or changes the signed in identity, we make no promises: undefined behavior ahead
1. iterate following feedback

Anyone else have thoughts?

Yes, `getattr` will retrieve the value of `Entity`'s `create` attribute, but it will not surface it to the caller of `create`. As is, `create` returns `None` like any default Python function.

A super simplistic example:
```
$ cat example.py
froop = "example"


def func(thing):
    getattr(thing, "__len__")


print(func(froop))
```
Gives us:
```
$ python example.py
None
```
Whereas:
```
$ cat example.py
froop = "example"


def func(thing):
    return getattr(thing, "__len__")


print(func(froop))
```
Gives us:
```
$ python example.py
<method-wrapper '__len__' of str object at 0x100a766c0>
```

It's not a great example, but it's easier/shorter than pasting here the entire module I have written around this.

Furthermore, my suggestion is consistent with every single other method in the library, please see:
* [`return`](https://github.com/PagerDuty/pagerduty-api-python-client/blob/master/pypd/models/incident.py#L44)
* [`return`](https://github.com/PagerDuty/pagerduty-api-python-client/blob/master/pypd/models/incident.py#L101)
* [`return`](https://github.com/PagerDuty/pagerduty-api-python-client/blob/master/pypd/models/incident.py#L123)
* [`return`](https://github.com/PagerDuty/pagerduty-api-python-client/blob/master/pypd/models/incident.py#L145)

To name just a few.

Even in the same file, `integration.py`, [`fetch`](https://github.com/PagerDuty/pagerduty-api-python-client/blob/master/pypd/models/integration.py#L48) implements this correctly. The expectation here is the same: create the resource, and return the object of the newly created resource. The only difference between the two functions is that `fetch` merely retrieves an assumed existing integration, and `create` asks to create it first.

And... we are good for a new journey in the wonderful "Windows, why ?" train!

So on Linux you have this really nice feature of shebang, that is read by the program loader and allows basically to treat any script as an executable as soon as you have the corresponding interpreter available on your system. 

There is no such things on Windows. The only files accepted by the program loader are pre-built binaries compiled against the win32 API, or Batch/Powershell scripts (well, you have dozens of supported extensions in fact but all lead to that two cases at the end).

This is the primary reason why people invented executable python wrappers like https://bitbucket.org/vinay.sajip/simple_launcher/src/default/, that are typically used by setuptools or distutil on windows. It consists in a fair pre-built binary executable as compiled against win32 API, _appended_ with the python script you want to execute in the very same file. The built logic self inspect its content, recognize the part corresponding to the python script, find the shebang in it, then launch the corresponding executable python launcher installed in the system to feed to it the embedded python script as the command argument. Magic.

What you are refering here has nothing to do with the process loader. It is a convenient shortcut to select the program you want to use and pass the given file to it when you double click on it. What will be done with the given file depends on the program you run: most of the time, it is about opening an editor (text, code, xls/doc) and start immediately the edition on that file. You have something similar with Gnome and other Linux window manager. But it has nothing to do with what will happen when you call `subprocess.Popen` that is mapped to the process launcher of the underlying system, and so have the limitation on Windows that I just described.

The `_transfer` function loops over all the statements in the block, so it is possible that you come across a `FunctionDef` node (nested function). However, The reason behind having this extra check is that a `Name` node could be the name of a function in a function call. For example the ast representation of  `print(x)` would be `Call(n=Name('print'),args=Name('x'))`. This means that the `name` variable in the code above could be storing `'print'`, a function name. It gets tricky when the function name is equivalent to a local variable name. In that case, there would be no difference (in the `Name` node) between a function name and a variable name.

Basing my judgement off https://github.com/encode/starlette/pull/804#issuecomment-590261001 here - Not entirely sure that's something that's actually possible at all, given the ASGI-interoperable design aspects of Starlette.

For example, if you have…

```python
async def other_non_starlette_app(scope, receive, send):
    ...

app = Starlette(routes=[Mount("/subapp", other_non_starlette_app)])
```

Then in this example context using `scope["routes"][-1]` as a way to get the "route that actually responds to the request" might be a bit confusing — it's only guaranteed to be the _last route within this app_ that handles the request _at least partly_.

Don't know if we want to play on words here, or if for the use case you're describing (manipulating route information for telemetry purposes) having partial route info in some cases and marking it as a limitation would be enough?

# Two "CPU"s are available.

This approach seems unnecessarily wasteful to me. Imagine that `len(taken_names) == N`. The next call to `generate_new_name` will take `O(N^2)` to generate a single new name:
- The `while` loop will only break out on iteration number `N + 1`.
- Since `taken_names` is a `Sequence`, the `in` check in the `while` loop is `O(N)`.

Why not use an infinite generator of new names, where each `next()` call on the generator produces a new, unique name? Or at the very least, why not make `taken_names` be a `Set`, and make the starting index be `len(taken_names)`?

Having a “count of failures” exit code is fairly common and certainly
reasonable, but when you do this you need to cap it at some value
because return codes wrap around after 256, so having exactly 256 errors
would be misclassified as passing. (Often I see this capped at 100 or
99; it’s good form to not return higher than 127, as those error codes
are also used by the shell to indicate signals.)

> maybe behind the scenes that's exactly what Python does 

Yep, default arguments are evaluated and stored when the function is defined, meaning default arguments are created once and re-used forever after.  The argument against mutable defaults is that changes in one function invocation persist to the next: 

```
In [18]: def foo(lst=[], ts=datetime.datetime.now()):
    ...:     lst.append(5)
    ...:     print(ts, lst)
    ...: 
    ...: 
    ...: foo()
    ...: time.sleep(1)
    ...: foo()
    ...: 
2020-02-24 10:52:10.714337 [5]
2020-02-24 10:52:10.714337 [5, 5]
```

So strictly speaking it's okay to have mutable defaults as long as you never mutate them.  But avoiding them at all will ensure you never accidentally mutate them by accident.

It's true that a ListArray64 can't be merged with a NumpyArray. The `merge` and `mergemany` functions are low-level and can only be used after `mergeable` certifies that the two arrays can be merged. If `mergeable` returns false, then `merge_as_union` must be used instead. The fact that the methods have these unwritten rules is why they're low-level: in Python, one should use `ak.concatenate`, and even most of our internal use of these functions are mediated through a `simplify` operation. I don't see any reason why this concatenate with `axis > 0` feature couldn't be implemented entirely by making a UnionArray and calling `simplify` on it.

I'm not sure if this is the right ratio here -- the shared fees (according to SIP-1) should be _T_ * _P_b_ where _P_b_ is the ratio of work done by this miner over the total work of miners in that window (and _T_ is the shared reward). This code calculates _T_ * (blocks_participated_in / number_of_blocks), which is a different ratio, and also problematic because user support burns, would allow many miners to participate in a single block, meaning that the split of shares wouldn't necessarily sum to 1, e.g., in a window of 2 blocks:

block 1 = mined by Alice, supported by Bob
block 2 = mined by Bob, supported by Alice

Both Alice and Bob would have num_blocks = 2, and thus each would claim 100% share of the total shared tx fee.

```
   output[output-term] = implicit-sum( input1[term1] * input2[term] * input3[term3])

When I implemented support for dataclasses, it was much simpler to implement the type conversion inside `translate_output_properties` rather than inside `deserialize_properties` (where you'd expect that code to live). So unfortunately, `translate_output_properties` is currently doing two things: 1. Translating key names (as it did originally), and 2. Translating the value to the target type, if specified. (Kinda gross, I know).

You can see inside the `invoke` call, all it's doing this:

https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi/blob/f22b47e54f39312b9b13ea359d3d523ee42e7a82/sdk/python/lib/pulumi/runtime/invoke.py#L125-L130

The `typ` parameter is an optional parameter to `invoke`. Our codegen for functions specifies `typ` when calling `invoke`, which takes care of translating the keys and instantiating the result class.

For example, in `pulumi_aws`, `get_bucket` does [this](https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi-aws/blob/2825d208fcf76bc890c5c2f5bb45921f896dc338/sdk/python/pulumi_aws/s3/get_bucket.py#L186):

```python
__ret__ = pulumi.runtime.invoke('aws:s3/getBucket:getBucket', __args__, opts=opts, typ=GetBucketResult).
```

`GetBucketResult` is decorated with `@pulumi.output_type`, so `translate_output_properties` will end up instantiating it and translating the keys based on the decorators on the class's properties.

In this particular case, for `getResource`, passing a `typ` to this invoke isn't going to work, because the target type is a resource, not an output type. So instead, I think we're going to need to do something along the lines of that code snippet in my earlier comment.

Basically call `types = _types.resource_types(type(res)) ` to get a dict of types for the resource, and then loop over the items in `state` translating the key with `res.translate_output_property(key)` and value with `translate_output_properties(value, res.translate_output_property, types.get(key)) `.

This could alternatively be:

```python
if tgt.get(ProvidesField).value is not None
```

or, to be more explicit:

```python
if tgt.get(ProvidesField, default_raw_value=None).value is not None
```

I went with first filtering via `tgt.has_field` as a premature optimization to avoid having `tgt.get` call the constructor `ProvidesField(raw_value=None, address=tgt.address)`. But, I realized that's not even much of a perf win due to other costs from this setup.

I'll simplify.

--

The takeaway is that `tgt.get(ProvidesField, None)` _does not_ return `Union[ProvidesField, None]`. Rather, `tgt.get(ProvidesField, default_raw_value=None)` returns `ProvidesField` because it calls `ProvidesField(raw_value=default_raw_value)` in the missing case.

I would like `LegacyAuthRequirementsErrorVariant` to be a protocol class which the other classes implement, but without providing any concrete methods.
Because `from_dict` is used in the `utils.py` module, it needs to be declared on the protocol, but the following is a suitable definition:

```python
class LegacyAuthRequirementsErrorVariant(Protocol):
    @classmethod
    def from_dict(cls: t.Type[T], error_dict: dict[str, t.Any]) -> T:
        ...

    def to_auth_requirements_error(self) -> GlobusAuthRequirementsError:
        ...
```

The main reason to prefer this declaration is that no inheritance is used, but type checkers will require that the type is matched in any assignment. For example, the following assignment requires (under type checkers) that the protocol is satisfied by the implementing class:
```python
x: type[LegacyAuthRequirementsErrorVariant] = LegacyConsentRequiredTransferError
```

This also separates out a question which I think should be answered differently:

I think we should make the implementations of `from_dict` and `to_dict` common for all of our classes. (I recognize that the classes in this module don't define `to_dict`, but I would have them inherit it.)
Rather than try to describe in detail, I'll just point to what I've done with `Serializable` in #796. Other names are fine, but I think sharing a common base for all of these helper objects which is defined _as_ a common base and is non-abstract leads to a better model.

Using "magical numbers" (= hard-coded number values) in code is not considered good practice for some reasons:
1. complicated to change, e.g. to add more significant digits (because I would need to re-calculate by hand)
2. error-prone (everyone reading your code would need to re-calculate to make sure, it is what the comment says)
3. we do not expect it here, but: if something in the original distribution changes, how can we make sure the correct values are used?

So I would propose the following:
* either calculate the distribution on the fly using its formula `log10(n + 1) - log10(n)`
* or calculate it once, store it into a constant outside of the function and use it here (preferred)

I wasn't too worried about breaking `LocationInfo` with an additive-only change, but my preference was also for doing things separately from `LocationInfo` just to keep that namedtuple immutable. This way, it gets created once and is never altered again, without any loss of expressive power and with minimal cost to ergonomics. If this feels un-ergonomic, we could easily write a helper method that takes a location and returns the combination of (Filter/Recurse/Location)Info data in whatever form we feel like is easiest to use.

I also think this metadata has uses far beyond `EXPLAIN` -- for example, we can use this same metadata together with a stream of writes for cache invalidation, or to evaluate whether to allow certain GraphQL queries based on a metadata-driven policy, etc.

Also, note that `RecurseInfo.depth` and `LocationInfo.recursive_scopes_depth` are not the same depth -- the first is the max depth of that recursion, whereas the second is the number of distinct `@recurse` directives that the current location is within (which has bearing on how the query is scheduled and executed).

In the way I've done it, there are theoretically very limited changes for people who aren't using auth (they are just not calling a global session anymore). ([Helpful TCP state transition reference](http://users.cs.northwestern.edu/~agupta/cs340/project2/TCPIP_State_Transition_Diagram.pdf))

Ok so here's how I understand the problem and solution:

Binding to port 0 makes the OS chose a random 'free' port. From the OS's perspective 'free' means any port that is in `CLOSED` state. 

In the previous implementation we simply bound to the port, grabbed the port number and closed it. That means it's immediately returned into the 'free' pool since its state will be `CLOSED` again.

Since we're running multiple things that require listening ports (and some require more than one port) it appears to me that sometimes the OS will re-use the same port number in successive iterations. 

By forcing the port into `TIME_WAIT` state (by connecting to the socket and then closing the connection) we effectively take it out of the OS's 'free' pool for the duration of that state (usually 60s).

By setting `SO_REUSEADDR` (and relying on the fact that most other 'server' kind of applications do the same) we however allow that port to be reused by another application (i.e. geth, synapse, etc.) while it's still in the `TIME_WAIT` state. AFAIU `SO_REUSEADDR` is independent of process group.

Regarding your question about splitting it. It basically already is. The name of the function is probably bad. It should really be called `get_free_port_generator` since it always returns a generator that will either return ports in increasing order (if you pass an `initial_port > 0`) or just random ports indefinitely.

I once lost 4 months on my primary computing server because of a problem with IT installing 4 separate copies of the backup server (without uninstalling the previous versions) and basically leaving my primary machine nonfunctional leaving 2 CPUs with 8 cores and the disk controller pegged 24-7.  

With 20k computers, the thought of one having a problem (or admitting that a central requirement has an issue) is at times challenging.   I am guessing that many people just don't notice their computer is dog slow and just put up with it (hence the perception everything is working fine).  I don't blame the local team as 90% of the time it isn't coming from them.  For example, the laptop came supplied with drivers and such from Dell, it was then reimaged to drivers and windows from 12 months ago.   Central are still evaluating the windows from 9 months ago, and that should be installed next month, at which time they will start evaluating the next version.  Yes, they backport all security fixes so we are bleeding edge on that front, but the actual image is always very out of date.  Real fun starts when their backport patch fails and the whole site as buggy or broken images for a month.   I can't imagine what life is like in our IT department, but  I would wager it is demoralizing at best.

We really haven't paid much attention to these levels.  We have three:
 * "experimental" is typically used for "don't use this, we might kill it".  For example, the worker-info information in the queue has this stability level, and we do intend to remove it.  Ideally we wouldn't use this level very often!
 * "stable" is typically used for "normal" API methods.  Almost all methods have this stability, except `reregisterWorker`, and I'm not sure why that's the case.
 * "deprecated" is used for methods we'd rather users didn't call, but which are still used.

So I think "stable" is the correct choice here (and IMHO `reregisterWorker` should be "stable" too).

Ahh, I expected this to be more flexible. It's better to keep this in a ``pyi`` file though. If one day a ``pyi`` is added for ``type_util`` a way is needed to prevent pytypes from self checking which would cause infinite recursion. ``@no_typecheck`` would be a way but that would also prevent static typechecking. pytypes could actively detect whether a to-be-checked function is from pytypes but that check slows down all typechecking and pytypes is already way too slow (because of such kind of checks etc). Having it in a ``pyi`` allows to have this detection in ``stubfile_manager``, i.e. it would simply not apply stubfiles from pytypes itself. This would keep the check out of the (non-initialization) runtime.
So, ``pyi`` is definitely the way to go.

The error message from Python 3 is quite explicit:
```python
from hashlib import sha1

hasher = sha1()
hasher.update("😜")
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
TypeError                                 Traceback (most recent call last)
<ipython-input-6-a47404780295> in <module>
----> 1 hasher.update("😜")

TypeError: Unicode-objects must be encoded before hashing
```

I also think it could be helpful for us to develop a concise migration guide going over issues like this for issues people are likely to run into! I'd be happy to work on that with people.

--

I do not think the right solution is us encoding things for them. Explicit is better than implicit, which is the whole point of Python 3 not allowing unicode here.

I'm also not sure we could even fix the compat issues for them even if we wanted to. Every implementation of `FingerprintStrategy` I've seen that does not use `stable_json_hash` explicitly calls `hashlib.sha1()`, and the issue is with the local definition of `compute_fingerprint()`, not our Pants logic. That is, the `TypeError` would surface before even hitting our code. The only workaround I'd see is catching `TypeError`, which feels very wrong.

Do you mean not concern themselves with `utf-8` vs. other encodings like `ascii`, or with having to encode to bytes to begin with?

My understanding is that there was a period where the SI prefixes meant jumps of 1000, unambiguously. Then it was decided that computers working in binary meant that we may as well use 1024 when talking about computers, which created ambiguity as context-dependent unit prefixes are not a good idea. So in \*hits wikipedia\* 1998, a standard was published giving binary prefixes their own letters, and thereby making SI units Standard again. k unambiguously means 1000, and Ki unambiguously means 1024, according to all major standards organisations and the SI system.

I fully accept that I am more nitpicky than most about this but I personally have strong feelings about maintaining correctness in this regard - in both the prefixes and the b/B. SI units are valuable because they are standardised and unambiguous. If people want to use them incorrectly, they're welcome to: that's the "consenting adults" ethos of python. We don't account for typos in file names either. I don't think the kilo/kibi will make much of a difference to anyone, but I'll add to the docstring to make it clear that proper capitalisation is expected.

For me, this change was about empowering users to write expressive, grok-able code, rather than saving keystrokes.

**My perspective:**
I called `send` and caught an exception. Next time I call `send`, it might be a different command, at a different time, on a different thread. I wouldn't want `send_packed_command` to flush my previous command at an arbitrary time in the future. I would settle for flushing a short "cancel sequence" instead, but RESP doesn't support that, so instead I'd like it to disconnect.

From that perspective, it's a RESP limitation.

**Your perspective:**
I called `send` and caught an exception. Now I want to retry immediately, but `send_packed_command` didn't tell me _where_ it stopped nor allows me to resume half-way, so I can't and it's a limitation of `send_packed_command`.

From that perspective, it's a `send_packed_command` limitation.

A couple reasons - this volume is being declared at build time, meaning it'll be wiped clean with every build and filled with the contents of that directory. It's the earliest implementation of Docker volumes and it's mostly superseded by the current run-time volume creation.

Generally, we're baking a lot of frequently-changed code during build time and at an early layer. Every rebuild would throw out the commands afterwards.

Runtime bind mounts would be more appropriate here (and in other cases I've described to you) because the cache wouldn't get invalidated by an early `COPY`. A later `COPY`  would preserve more cache, but I don't see a benefit in baking `src` files into the image if they don't need to be there (like if we were using the image for a K8s cluster or something). 

We're using docker for the sole purpose of environment isolation here: why fill the image with files that aren't necessary for environment creation?

Some time long ago we had `shallowcopy()` (which copied a reference to Column's buffer), and `deepcopy()`, which copied the buffer's content.
Today, `deepcopy()` no longer exists -- the buffer is copied automatically and only when necessary. But we added a "standard" copy too: `Column(const Column&)` which is even "shallower" than `shallowcopy()`. Thus, in order to avoid possible misunderstandings, I renamed `shallowcopy()` into `clone()`.

The `DataTable::copy()` method could be renamed into `clone()` as well, I don't see any contradiction there. Right now, however, its name mimics python's `Frame.copy()` (which in turns follows standard python naming conventions).

The problem with modifying and restoring class attribute is that if an exception occurs somewhere between modifying and restoring the value, then we will get a modified object:
<details><summary>Code example</summary>

```python
class Example:
    def __init__(self):
        self.attr = "initial value"
    
    def method(self):
        previous_value = self.attr
        self.attr = "modified value"
        self._private_method()
        self.attr = previous_value
    
    def _private_method(self):
        raise Exception

obj = Example()
print(obj.attr)
try:
    obj.method()
except:
    pass
print(obj.attr)

# Output:
# initial value
# modified value
```

</details>

I described it details in the corresponding ticket, in the https://github.com/localstack/localstack/issues/2432#issuecomment-640158726

As for `str` and `bytes`, let me clarify that argument is always a string:
The argument is always _encoded_ string (in your example, the third case `"string".encode("UTF-8")`). Apologies, I misunderstood your initial note. 
The fact that wrongly delivered binary payload - is the correct payload, that got encoded the second time shows the direction of the fix. 

In my understanding the flow is the following: 
1) The client sends the request to SNS with aws library that makes encoding of binary message attribute;
2) LocalStack gets the request and converts messages from SNS to SQS with boto library by copying message attributes; But at this point, the binary attribute is already base64 encoded string.
3) LocalStack sends SQS requests with boto, and the boto library makes the encoding of binary attributes again.

To avoid second encoding, binary attributes should be set as bytearray during SNS message to SQS message conversion.

Ah! This one is tricky. I would say even that is it a master of trickery, or a trickyception.

First, we have several tests, `certbot-auto` tests in particular, where we check the behavior of `certbot-auto` with and without interactive terminal. We do some tricks in these tests to make `certbot-auto` believe it is running or not in an interactive terminal. This is the first level of tricks.

But to make this work, the tests scripts must start in an interactive terminal. Since there are inside dockers, we use `docker run -it` to make the script thinks it is in an interactive terminal (while a simple `docker run` would make the execution non interactive). This is the second level of tricks.

However, if the place where `docker run -it` is not a proper terminal, you will receive a `the input device is not a TTY` error while trying to execute it. For instance, try `echo "Hello" | docker run -it --rm ubuntu:18.04 echo World!`.

This what is happening in Azure Pipeline, which is executing the bash commands in a non-interactive mode. Which makes sense, since you cannot input things in a CI job. Travis was simulating in some way a terminal for these commands, so it was working.

This is the third level of tricks. Initially I used `unbuffer`, from the `expect` package, and used it for Linux jobs directly to invoke the `tox` command. So the "Run tox" step in `tox-steps.yml` was actually invoking `unbuffer tox` when `unbuffer` is available (and that is why `expect` was installed, as noticed above). Indeed `unbuffer` call has the side effect to make the binary/script believe it is running in a interactive mode. At least for `docker run -it`. This corresponds to the run https://dev.azure.com/certbot/certbot/_build/results?buildId=2064&view=results.

However I thought it was not elegant, because it was specific to Azure Pipeline, to fixing the behavior in other situations where `tox` would be called for these tests in a non-interactive mode, and because the trick was a side effect of `unbuffer`. Then I found `script` which has this side effect as an "official" one, describe it its documentation. As an additional benefit `script` comes from `util-linux`, which is an almost always installed package in a strandard Linux distribution. 

So I wanted to use it one or two level below, to make all calls benefit from it, not the specific situation of Azure. Sadly the best place, which is directly inside the `CMD` instruction of the Dockerfile, is not possible because CentOS6 does not have `util-linux`, but only `util-linux-ng` which install the BSD version, with less possibilities (in particular, transfering the exit code of the callee). So I put it one layer up, in `tox.ini`, so any call of `tox` will continue to benefit from it. As a side note, `unbuffer` can be installed in CentOS 6, but sadly (again) is not sufficient to trick the `certbot-auto` scripts ...

However, considering that it adds some complexity, and break for MacOS (with no straightforward solution instead a manual script implementation), nobody ever complained about it, I think I will just keep things simple, and call `script` from `tox-steps.yml`.

I didn't turn return values into references because (a) downstream users might modify my class's internal data (though maybe that could be prevented with `const std::vector<const std::string>`?) and (b) downstream users might own these data longer than my class exists. Case (b) can look like valid code but randomly segfault, and I don't even want to go there, especially for the limited value of passing a list of strings to Python more quickly (which Python will then use slowly). Rather than make this decision independently each time, I have a blanket policy of always returning copies. That's why there are methods like `variable_at(int index)` to get a variable's value quickly, rather than getting a copy of the whole vector just to pull out one value.

Version-pinning for libraries is a really tricky issue. Non-semver libs have to be handled case-by-case, but we can reason systematically about semver libs. And `typing-extensions` is semantically versioned as of v4+. Here's my argument, which I'm still evolving as I think through this (so I appreciate any counterpoint):

- For any given semver library, we're often going to need to pin above a major version `x`. This is often a reasonable constraint to place on users _if_ `x` has significant adoption-- it's a case-by-case judgment call.
- A rational library author won't pin a semver library below a minor (i.e. non-breaking) version-- that's the point of semver.
- It follows that, if we agree it's OK to pin a semver lib above major version `x`, then, _assuming the libs with which dagster will cohabit are rationally pinning_, it is OK to pin the same semver lib above minor version `x.y`.

Mapped to the current question: if it's OK to require `typing-extensions` be v4+, _then_ it should be OK to require `4.1.1+`, assuming cohabiting libs are pinning rationally (I'll address this below).

Is it reasonable to expect users to be using `typing-extensions` 4+? Yes. The only reason to use below v4 is if you are using Python 3.5 (v4 dropped some stuff that is in 3.6 stdlib). Python 3.5 is end-of-life, if it we don't support it for Dagster itself, it's OK to require dependency versions that don't support it.

OK, so what about the next logical step? Is it OK to require 4.1.1 if we require 4+? In an ideal world where every lib that claims to be semver really is 100% semver, and all cohabiting libraries are rationally pinning, the answer is straightforwardly yes. Are we close to that ideal world in the case of`typing-extensions`?

I think close enough. Here's a blurb from the `typing-extensions` README:

>Starting with version 4.0.0, typing_extensions uses [Semantic Versioning](https://semver.org/). The major version is incremented for all backwards-incompatible changes. Therefore, it's safe to depend on typing_extensions like this: typing_extensions >=x.y, <(x+1), where x.y is the first version that includes all features you need.

Now, `dbt-core` _was_ irrationally capping `typing-extensions` at an arbitrary version under 4, but when [multiple users had problems](https://github.com/dbt-labs/dbt-core/issues/4833#event-6293797270), they raised the cap, and when I asked the dev why they were capping at all, they didn't know and so dropped it totally. That's coming out in a patch release. `tensorflow` also had an irrational cap in place that they recently dropped. I think any other commonly used libs out there that irrationally cap will drop it eventually or at request, given that these caps serve no purpose and reliably trigger problems for lots of users.

>This would mean that users who can't for whatever reason upgrade to the latest dbt-core also can't use newer versions of dagster if we land this right?

This is true, but does it matter for patch and/or minor releases of semver libs (e.g. `dbt-core`)?). It seems reasonable to me for Dagster minor releases to require the latest patch releases of semver libs (again, because there is no reason to pin below a patch). But maybe you disagree?

`rgb_from_hdx` and `hdx_from_rgb` are constants, and so are the two arrays I replaced them with: https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/blob/ff188272a0ea509cb4d6025d1906c494914a4304/skimage/color/colorconv.py#L485

There's a long list of these arrays there. These arrays describe the RGB color of each of the stains in a common set. If you want to test that these arrays are correct, you'd have to source an image of a slide stained with the corresponding stains, apply the stain unmixing, and verify that there are few negative values in the result.

It is the functions [`separate_stains`](https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/blob/ff188272a0ea509cb4d6025d1906c494914a4304/skimage/color/colorconv.py#L1391) and [`combine_stains`](https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/blob/ff188272a0ea509cb4d6025d1906c494914a4304/skimage/color/colorconv.py#L1459) that we're testing here with these round-trips. Both the HED<->RGB and the BRO<->RGB round-trips test the same two functions, just with different data.

If you really want to test these functions with each of the data matrices, we could certainly do that. For the 2-stain varieties, we'd have to compare only the first two channels after the roundtrip. Or set the 3rd channel of the input to 0. I just don't think that such a test would add anything here.

This is risky because it assumes too much about how the local proxies are configured.

It's possible that no proxy is in use, or other proxies may exist outside of your control. Even if you're aware of the local network infra, nefarious clients could prepend this, and since there is no clear standard for how to implement XFF, it's not clear what the reverse proxy server _should_ do here.

For example,

```http
GET /restricted-resource
Host: vulnerable.host
X-Forwarded-For: fake.ip.address, real.ip,address
```

From the [wiki article](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/X-Forwarded-For)

>  for this reason, effective use of XFF requires knowledge of which proxies are trustworthy, for instance by looking them up in a whitelist of servers whose maintainers can be trusted. 

In general, you want the right-most IP address that you don't recognize. 

```py
# settings.py
# proxies that exist in my infrastructure
KNOWN_PROXIES = [
    '10.1.2.3',
    '10.1.2.4',
    '10.1.2.5',
]

# middleware.py
class XFFMiddleware():
    def __call__(self, request):
        xff = request.META['HTTP_X_FORWARDED_FOR']
        ips = [ip.strip() for ip in xff.split(',')]
        for ip in reversed(ips):
            if ip not in settings.KNOWN_PROXIES:
                request.META['REMOTE_ADDR'] = ip
                break
        return self.get_response(request)
```

Whether we use 1.5 or 1.3 is mute if we do not scale to the logical problem.
Define logical problem as 
H = sum_{i<j } Jij s_i s_j + sum_i h_i s_i (matches BQM definition). 
Define 
RMS(J) = [sum_{i<j} J_ij^2]/[N(N-1)/2].  Note that num_edges= N(N-1)/2. 
We should define chain strength as 
CS = A RMS(J) sqrt(N) 

A = 1.5 is a good choice for spin glasses on Bay2 (DW2000Q)
A = 1.2 is a better choice for Bay17 (Advantage)
Either choice is much better than the current default.

On runtime, invalid annotation like `BytesConevrtable: TypeAlias = NotExistentThing  # type: ignore` will be even worse then. The type ignore will suppress any issues from pyright, including the one about `NotExistentThing` not existing, and since this would be in the `TYPE_CHECKING` block, it won't even run on runtime, meaning no errors will show up.

Yes, by making it a string we won't get runtime exceptions if the class isn't defined, but because we use `__future__.annotations`, we wouldn't get these from annotated function parameters anyway, and it's not an issue to make type aliases act similarly. Type ignore is way more problematic here and I think simply making it a string makes much more sense.

1) We can keep things like this
2) We can have two dedicated methods, one using `subprocess.check_call, another using `subprocess.check_output` and call appropriately

I see you are still using `"host": "cloud.mindsdb.com"`, and thus, connecting MindsDB to itself.

The idea is that you have your own MySQL database (here is how you can install MySQL database: https://dev.mysql.com/doc/mysql-getting-started/en/#mysql-getting-started-installing).

And then you connect this database to MindsDB:
```
CREATE DATABASE mysql_test                  --- datasource connection name
WITH ENGINE = 'mysql',                      --- database engine
PARAMETERS = {
    "host": "localhost",            --- host name can be an IP address, or URL
    "port": 3306,                           --- port used to make TCP/IP connection
    "database": "dry_bean_db",              --- database name
    "user": "demo-user",                    --- database user
    "password": "demo-password"             --- database password
};
```

If you use local database, you may need to [setup an ngrok channel](https://docs.mindsdb.com/sql/create/database#making-your-local-database-available-to-mindsdb) and use provided values for host and port.

I do agree that this is borderline overkill. Each of these methods is small enough that there's not much gained with the abstraction.

To get this through approval, it sounds like you're proposing two possible options:
1. just inline the code
2. add test cases for each of the functions

I'd personally prefer (2), because IMO the Pulumi codebase _does_ have too many long method, particularly in codegen, that make it hard to test, and it's better to risk rubberbanding to the other extreme to make sure this function doesn't become unmanageable overtime. 

> humans are pretty well adapted to reading long form text, we're not so good at jumping from reference to reference

I think tiny functions aren't optimizing for the case where you jump to them, but the case where you _don't_. 90% of the time someone is reading this function, they're not going to need to jump to `setVersion` because there's probably something else in this method that's actually causing the problem. So by abstracting the internals of `setVersion`, I'm removing those details from the mix when someone is tracing something of actual merit.

Nevertheless, I think I'll just go ahead and inline these tiny functions because you're right that it's at least questionably overkill. XD

So I'm going to go ahead and argue the case that `setup_for_execution` should be invoked outside of the `try`. That would mean that `teardown_after_execution` would _not_ be called if `setup_for_execution` throws. 

In effect this would say that if `setup_for_execution` fails, the resource is in an invalid, untrusted state and will not be invoked by the framework.

On a practical basis, this prevents one from having to write a bunch of boilerplate in teardown methods to prevent crashes if the setup fails.

Imagine the following class:
```
class ResourceWithComplexSetup(ConfigurableResource):
    # ...
    def setup_for_execution(self):
        self._thing_one = get_thing_one()
        self._thing_two = get_thing_two()
```

Now imagine that and `get_thing_one`, and `get_thing_two` can both throw.

Now the user has to deal with many states of the object. Namely that both `_thing_one` and `_thing_two` are None (`get_thing_one` threw), `_thing_two` is None ('get_thing_two' threw), or neither is None

In order to do thing safely at minimum you have to ensure that each object is not None to account for the three possible states of the object.
```
class ResourceWithComplexSetup(ConfigurableResource):
    # ...
    def teardown_after_execution(self):
        if self._thing_one:
            self._thing_one.cleanup()
        if self._thing_two:
           self._thing_two.cleanup()
```
This obviously can be much more complex. There are more subtle cases in this when the code in the setup methods are modifying some global state in some way. You have to think about the combination of all the possible codepaths and think about all the possible states the object might be in to handle teardown safely 

It's worth noting that we have an escape hatch in _either_ case, in that we can instruct the user to override `yield_for_execution` if the default we choose does not suit them.  They can put setup code inside or outside of the `try` block they write themselves. (The purist in me wants to only have this single plug-ability point, to simplify the relationship between framework code and user code [avoiding the conversation about the semantics of teardown entirely], but it would make the simple cases a bit annoying).

I see what you mean, but there is a few issues of removing the `lambda` in the `minimize`:

1) The warmup phase is a maximization problem and the second phase is a minimization problem, we could unify this behaviour and transform both problems in a minimization problem. ✅ 

2) To remove the `gp` and `y_max` from the function we would need to introduce a new lambda/function in the "else" part

```python
    if constraint is not None:

        def adjusted_ac(x, gp, y_max):
            (...)
            return values * p_constraints
    else:
        adjusted_ac = lambda x: -ac(x, gp=gp, y_max=y_max)
```

I guess one advantage is that the `minimize` part do many calls and the warmup phase does only a single call.

3) We would also need to conditionally `.reshape(1, -1` inside the `adjusted_ac`, because the `minimize` passes a 1D array if I'm not mistaken.

```python
    if constraint is not None:

        def adjusted_ac(x, gp, y_max):
            """Acquisition function adjusted to fulfill the constraint when necessary"""
            if len(x.shape) == 1:
                x = x.reshape(1, -1)

            values = ac(x, gp=gp, y_max=y_max)
            (...)
            return values * p_constraints

    else:
        adjusted_ac = lambda x: -ac(x.reshape(1, -1) if len(x.shape) == 1 else x, gp=gp, y_max=y_max)
```

I don't think that this is going to do quite what you want. The comment on `Goal.IgnoreChanges` is unfortunately misleading (PR to update it is [here](https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi/pull/8491)): its contents are a list of property paths, not just a list of top-level property keys. [`processIgnoreChanges`](https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi/blob/master/pkg/resource/deploy/step_generator.go#L1164-L1201) has an example of how to work with these paths. I think that the most straightforward way to get this right is to call `processIgnoreChanges` _again_ on `oldInputs` and `newInputs` prior to computing the diff (`processIgnoreChanges` has already been run once using the original inputs from the program; rerunning is necessary b/c `newInputs` is the result of `Check` and may differ from the original inputs due to e.g. default application by the provider). This will also ensure that we don't return `DiffSome` if all changes are ignored.

I can certainly move the driver in that direction. Originally, the `position` property was only used to get/set the value of the controller's internal position reference. It wouldn't actually move the motor at all. Though I think being able to do `motor.position = 1234` and have the motor move to that position would be quite nice. I'd also like to distinguish between _step position_ and _absolute position_. The motor controller always counts position measured in steps, though in my use case I'd like to be able to command an _absolute_ position in say meters, inches, etc. 

A potential way this can be accomplished is to have two properties, one called `step_position` which behaves exactly like the current `position` property but with the added feature that you can run `motor.step_position = <desired position>` and the motor will actually move to that position. The other property would be called `absolute_position` and it also behaves exactly the same way, but calls unit conversion methods `absolute_to_steps()` when setting a new position and `steps_to_absolute()` when querying position. These methods would of course have to be implemented in a subclass as the mapping between steps and absolute position will vary depending on what the user is driving with the controller. What do you think of this type of interface? My next commit will include my idea for this implementation.

Hi @gavishpoddar, the `LANGUAGE_DETECTION_CONFIDENCE_THRESHOLD` can take values between 0 and 1, but you are allowing any value.


You have to add here an "extra_check" to check the values:

```python
'LANGUAGE_DETECTION_CONFIDENCE_THRESHOLD': {
    'type': float,
    'extra_check': _check_between_0_and_1
},

```

Then, you can define the `_check_between_0_and_1` (after the `_check_parsers` function):

```python
def _check_between_0_and_1(setting_name, setting_value):
    is_valid = 0 <= setting_value <= 1
    if not is_valid:
        raise SettingValidationError(
            '{} is not a valid value for {}. It can take values between 0 and '
            '1.'.format(
                setting_value, setting_name,
            )
        )
```

    ):

```
